title: Day 10 Quiz — Lists, Filtering, Keys, and React.memo
description: Verify array chaining, product types, list keys, and React.memo performance basics.

questions.prompt: Array.prototype.sort() returns a new array without modifying the original.
questions.explanation: sort mutates the array in place. Copy with spread or slice before sorting state arrays.

questions.prompt: Why use product.id as key instead of array index?

options.text: Indexes are slower to compare
Index changes when list is sorted or filtered, breaking React reconciliation
React requires numeric keys
Indexes cause TypeScript errors

questions.explanation: Stable unique keys let React match the same item across reorders. Index keys misidentify components after sort/filter.

options.text: Caches API responses
Skips re-render if props are shallowly equal to previous render
Memoizes all hooks inside the component
Prevents state updates

questions.explanation: memo wraps a component and bails out of re-render when props haven't changed.

questions.prompt: Which defeat React.memo optimization? Select all that apply.

options.text: Passing inline arrow function as prop every render
Passing product object with same reference
Passing inline object literal as prop every render
Passing primitive string title prop unchanged

questions.explanation: New function/object references every render fail shallow compare. Stable primitives and same object references allow memo to skip.

questions.prompt: Use {{blank1}} to derive filteredProducts from products and filter state without recomputing every render. Chain {{blank2}} and sort on a copied array.
questions.explanation: useMemo caches derived list; filter returns new arrays without mutation.

questions.prompt: What is the length of result?
questions.code:
const products = [{ id: 1, price: 100 }, { id: 2, price: 300 }, { id: 3, price: 200 }];
const result = products.filter(p => p.price >= 150).map(p => p.id);
questions.explanation: Products with price 300 and 200 pass the filter — two items.

questions.prompt: Client-side filter vs Day 09 server search — when is client-side appropriate?

options.text: Always — server search is obsolete
When dataset is small enough to load once and filter in memory
Only for static HTML sites
Never — always paginate on server

questions.explanation: Small catalogs filter client-side for instant UX. Large datasets need server-side search and pagination.

questions.prompt: Why should you copy an array before calling sort() on React state data?
questions.sampleAnswer: sort mutates in place. React state must be treated as immutable — mutating state arrays directly skips change detection and causes bugs. Copy with [...arr] or arr.slice() first.
questions.explanation: Immutability discipline prepares for Day 12 cart updates.
memo from React wraps the component for shallow prop comparison.

questions.prompt: What happens if you use Math.random() as list key?

options.text: Better performance
React remounts every item every render — state and focus lost
Keys become more unique over time
Nothing — keys are optional

questions.explanation: Random keys change every render. React treats each item as new — full unmount/remount cycle.
